summary: I was born and raised in Stockton, California, where my family has deep roots. Growing up there gave me a typical childhood for the time, and my parents instilled strong values and a high regard for education. Stockton was the foundation of my early life and shaped my initial outlook before I moved on to pursue higher education.

I chose to attend the University of California, Berkeley because it was a prestigious institution close to home with a strong engineering program. My time there was formative, especially as I participated in the V-12 Navy program during World War II. Later, I returned to Berkeley as a faculty member in the College of Engineering, where I developed my academic career and took on administrative roles.

During World War II, I was part of the V-12 Navy College Training Program, which was designed to train officers quickly. This program gave me discipline and a strong sense of purpose while I was still a student. It was a critical part of my military service and helped shape my leadership skills.

World War II was a defining period in my life. I served in the United States Naval Reserve and was part of the 125th Naval Construction Battalion stationed in Okinawa. The war taught me leadership and resilience, which influenced my post-war academic and professional plans.

I enlisted in the United States Naval Reserve during World War II and participated in the V-12 program to become an officer. My service included deployment with the 125th Naval Construction Battalion in Okinawa. This experience was instrumental in developing my leadership abilities and discipline.

I served in the 125th Naval Construction Battalion during World War II, which was stationed in Okinawa. This unit was part of my military experience in the Naval Reserve, and it taught me valuable lessons in leadership and resilience. The time with the battalion had a lasting impact on my life and career.

During World War II, I was deployed to Okinawa as a member of the 125th Naval Construction Battalion. Serving there was a challenging experience that taught me resilience and leadership. Okinawa was a significant location in my military service and shaped my perspective on duty and perseverance.

I pursued my Ph.D. at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign in Theoretical and Applied Mechanics. It was a rigorous and rewarding program where I worked with outstanding faculty and fellow students. During this time, I also got married and started a family, marking a period of both personal and academic growth.

entity: College of Engineering, University of California, Berkeley
summary: After completing my Ph.D., I returned to the College of Engineering at UC Berkeley as a faculty member. I started as a lecturer and gradually took on more responsibilities in research and administration. Eventually, I became dean of the College, focusing on supporting diversity, fundraising for new facilities, and fostering industry relationships.

entity: Wason Medal for Most Meritorious Paper
summary: One of the highlights of my academic career was receiving the Wason Medal for Most Meritorious Paper alongside my colleague Boris Bresler. This award recognized our research contributions in structural mechanics and viscoelasticity. It was a proud moment that reflected the hard work and collaboration we invested in our projects.

Boris Bresler was my colleague with whom I collaborated on research that earned us the Wason Medal for Most Meritorious Paper. Working with Boris was a rewarding experience, and together we contributed to advancements in structural mechanics. Our partnership was an important part of my academic journey at Berkeley.

I spent a sabbatical year at University College Cork in Ireland as a Fulbright lecturer. This experience was enriching both professionally and personally, allowing me to engage with faculty and students there. My family and I traveled extensively during that time, and I gained new perspectives on engineering education and research.

Through the Fulbright Program, I had the opportunity to lecture at University College Cork for a year. This program facilitated my international academic exchange and broadened my professional horizons. It was a valuable experience that enriched my understanding of global engineering education.

Later in my career, I served as chancellor at the University of California, Santa Cruz. This administrative role allowed me to influence higher education beyond Berkeley and address broader university challenges. It was a significant leadership position that complemented my earlier academic and administrative experiences.

entity: Computer Science building, University of California, Berkeley
summary: As dean of the College of Engineering at UC Berkeley, one of my priorities was fundraising for new facilities, including the Computer Science building. Supporting infrastructure development was crucial to advancing research and education in engineering and computer science. This project reflected my commitment to fostering growth and innovation at Berkeley.
